title: Building a RAG-powered document Q&A app with MongoDB
description:
Start by setting up a document ingestion pipeline. Use PyPDF or Unstructured to parse uploaded documents into text, then chunk the text using recursive character splitting (1000 chars, 200 overlap). Generate embeddings for each chunk using Voyage AI's embedding model and store them in a MongoDB collection with fields for chunk_text, embedding, source_file, page_number, and upload_timestamp. Create a MongoDB Vector Search index on the embedding field with cosine similarity.

For the query flow, embed the user's question with the same Voyage AI model, run $vectorSearch with numCandidates set to 10x your desired limit, then pass the top-k chunks as context to your LLM API with a system prompt instructing the model to answer only from the provided context. Build the frontend with React, adding a file upload component that triggers ingestion and a chat interface that streams responses using the LLM's streaming API. Deploy the backend on Railway with the MongoDB Atlas connection string as an environment variable.

Pitfalls to avoid: Chunk size matters more than most other decisions — too small and you lose context, too large and you dilute relevance. Always use the same embedding model for indexing and querying. Don't skip metadata filtering — as the document collection grows, add $match after $vectorSearch to scope results by source file or date range. Stream the LLM response to the frontend rather than waiting for completion — the perceived latency difference is significant.

title: Building a customer support agent with tool use and conversation memory
description:
Set up the agent loop using your LLM's tool use API. Define tools for the agent's capabilities: lookup_order (queries MongoDB orders collection by order_id or customer email), check_inventory (reads from products collection), issue_refund (writes to refunds collection and updates order status), and escalate_ticket (creates a document in the escalations collection). Each tool function executes a MongoDB query and returns structured results.

For conversation memory, store each message in a conversations collection with session_id, role, content, tool_use metadata, and timestamp. On each new user message, load the last 20 messages for the session as conversation history into the LLM API call. For cross-session memory, extract key facts after each resolved conversation (customer preferences, past issues, resolution outcomes) and store them in a customer_memory collection with the customer's ID. Retrieve these at session start using a simple find() and inject them into the system prompt. Build the frontend as a chat widget embeddable in the existing support portal, with WebSocket connections for real-time message streaming.

Pitfalls to avoid: Tool definitions must have precise descriptions — vague descriptions cause the model to pick the wrong tool. Always validate tool inputs before executing MongoDB queries (especially user-supplied IDs) to prevent injection. Set a max tool-use loop count (e.g., 10 iterations) to prevent runaway agent loops. Don't store full tool results in conversation history sent back to the API — summarize large query results to stay within context limits.

title: Building a real-time collaborative dashboard with MongoDB Change Streams
description:
Design the data layer first. Create a MongoDB collection for dashboard widgets, each document containing widget_id, dashboard_id, widget_type (chart, metric, table), query_config (the aggregation pipeline that powers it), position (grid coordinates), and last_updated. Create a separate metrics collection using MongoDB time-series format for the underlying data, with timeField set to recorded_at and metaField set to metric_name.

For the backend, set up a FastAPI server with WebSocket endpoints. When a client connects to a dashboard, open a MongoDB change stream filtered to that dashboard_id. On each change event, push the updated widget data to all connected clients via WebSocket. For the widget data itself, run the stored aggregation pipeline from query_config against the metrics collection and cache results in a Redis layer with a 30-second TTL to avoid re-running expensive aggregations on every connection. Build the React frontend with a grid layout library (react-grid-layout) for drag-and-drop widget positioning. Each widget component subscribes to its WebSocket channel and re-renders on incoming data. Save position changes back to MongoDB via a debounced PATCH endpoint.

Pitfalls to avoid: Don't open one change stream per widget — open one per dashboard and demux on the client side. Change streams hold a cursor on the server, so too many streams will exhaust connection limits. Use motor (async driver) with FastAPI, not PyMongo. Cache aggressively — recalculating aggregations on every change event will overload the cluster. Add a heartbeat ping on the WebSocket to detect stale connections and close their associated change streams.

title: Building a multimodal search app with image and text retrieval
description:
Set up a MongoDB collection for the asset library with fields for title, description, file_url, content_type (image/text/pdf), text_embedding, image_embedding, tags, and created_at. For text content, generate embeddings using Voyage AI's text embedding model. For images, use Voyage AI's multimodal embedding model to generate embeddings from the image directly. Store both embedding types as separate fields in the same document.

Create two MongoDB Vector Search indexes — one on text_embedding and one on image_embedding. Build the search API with two query paths: text-to-text (embed the query with the text model, search text_embedding) and text-to-image (embed the query with the multimodal model, search image_embedding). Combine results using reciprocal rank fusion — take the top results from each path, assign scores based on rank position, merge and sort by combined score. Build the frontend with a search bar that returns a mixed grid of text and image results, with preview cards that adapt layout based on content_type. Add filtering by tags and date range using $match stages after $vectorSearch.

Pitfalls to avoid: Text and image embeddings live in different vector spaces — don't search one index with an embedding generated for the other. Reciprocal rank fusion is simple and effective but tune the k parameter (default 60) based on your result quality. Store thumbnails alongside original images to keep the search results page fast. Don't embed and index on upload synchronously — use a background job queue so the upload endpoint returns immediately.

title: Building a real-time anomaly detection dashboard with streaming ingestion
description:
Set up a MongoDB time-series collection for sensor/event data with timeField as event_time, metaField as source_id, and granularity set to seconds or minutes based on your ingestion rate. Create a FastAPI endpoint that accepts batched event payloads and inserts them with insert_many for throughput. For anomaly detection, create a scheduled Atlas Trigger that runs every minute: it executes an aggregation pipeline computing rolling averages and standard deviations per source_id over the last hour using $setWindowFields, flags data points beyond 3 standard deviations, and writes anomalies to a separate anomalies collection.

For the real-time frontend, open a change stream on the anomalies collection. When a new anomaly is inserted, push it to connected dashboard clients via WebSocket. Build the dashboard in React with a time-series chart (use Recharts or Chart.js with streaming plugin) showing the live data feed and anomaly markers overlaid as colored dots. Add a side panel listing recent anomalies with source, severity, and timestamp, auto-updating via the WebSocket feed. Store anomaly acknowledgment status in the anomaly document so operators can mark them as reviewed.

Pitfalls to avoid: Time-series collections are optimized for ordered inserts — inserting events out of timestamp order degrades performance. Don't run the anomaly detection aggregation against the full collection each time — use $match to window it to the relevant time range first. $setWindowFields is powerful but memory-intensive on large windows; keep the rolling window as small as your detection logic allows. Add a TTL index on raw events (e.g., 7 days) but keep anomalies longer for audit purposes.

title: Building an AI-powered content moderation pipeline
description:
Create a MongoDB collection for user-submitted content with fields for content_id, content_type (text/image), raw_content, moderation_status (pending/approved/flagged/rejected), moderation_result, submitted_at, and reviewed_at. Set up a processing pipeline triggered by new submissions: use a change stream on the content collection filtered to moderation_status: 'pending'.

For text moderation, send the content to your LLM API with a system prompt that classifies content into categories (safe, needs_review, violation) and returns structured JSON with the category, confidence score, and reasoning. For image moderation, use a vision-capable model by passing the image as a base64 content block in the LLM API call with the same classification prompt. Write the moderation result back to the original document and update moderation_status. Route 'needs_review' items to a human review queue — build this as a simple React admin panel that queries the content collection filtered by moderation_status: 'needs_review', sorted by submitted_at. Human reviewers update the status, which triggers downstream actions (publish or notify user). Create a MongoDB Search index on moderation_result.reasoning to let moderators search past decisions for consistency.

Pitfalls to avoid: Don't moderate synchronously in the upload request — it adds 2-5 seconds of latency. Use the change stream pattern to process asynchronously and show 'pending' status in the UI. Always store the full moderation reasoning, not just the label — you'll need it for appeals and auditing. Set a timeout on the LLM API call and default to 'needs_review' if it fails, rather than auto-approving. Batch image processing carefully — large base64 payloads consume significant tokens.

title: Building a real-time multiplayer quiz app with live leaderboard
description:
Design three MongoDB collections: quizzes (quiz_id, title, questions array with options and correct_answer), sessions (session_id, quiz_id, host_user, status, started_at, participants array), and responses (session_id, user_id, question_index, selected_answer, is_correct, response_time_ms, submitted_at). Use response_time_ms for tiebreaking on the leaderboard.

Build the backend with FastAPI and WebSocket rooms. When a host creates a session, generate a join code and store the session document. Participants join via the code, which adds them to the participants array using $addToSet. The host triggers each question — broadcast it to all connected clients via WebSocket. Clients submit answers via WebSocket, which the server validates, writes to the responses collection, and acknowledges. After each question, run an aggregation pipeline on responses for that session: $group by user_id, $sum correct answers, $avg response_time_ms, then $sort by score descending and response time ascending. Broadcast the updated leaderboard to all clients. Build the React frontend with a lobby screen (waiting for players), a question screen (timer + options), and a leaderboard screen (animated rank transitions between questions). Use a MongoDB change stream on the sessions collection to update the lobby in real time as players join.

Pitfalls to avoid: Don't compute the leaderboard client-side — different clients will disagree on scores if a response write hasn't propagated. Keep it server-authoritative. Use $addToSet not $push for the participants array to handle duplicate join attempts. WebSocket rooms need cleanup — remove disconnected players and close change streams on session end. Add server-side answer validation with a submission deadline per question to prevent late answers from counting.

title: Building a personalized AI newsletter generator with scheduled delivery
description:
Create MongoDB collections for users (user_id, email, interests array, preferred_frequency, preferred_tone, last_delivery_at), content_sources (source_id, source_url, source_type, last_fetched_at), and raw_articles (article_id, source_id, title, content, published_at, embedding). Set up a scheduled job that fetches new content from sources, chunks and embeds articles using Voyage AI, and stores them in the raw_articles collection with a vector search index on the embedding field.

For newsletter generation, create a scheduled Atlas Trigger that runs based on each user's preferred_frequency. For each eligible user: run $vectorSearch against raw_articles using the user's interests as the query, filtered to articles published since last_delivery_at. Take the top 8-10 articles and pass them to your LLM API with a system prompt that generates a newsletter in the user's preferred_tone, summarizing each article with a key takeaway and link. Store the generated newsletter in a newsletters collection and send it via an email service (SendGrid or Resend). Update last_delivery_at on the user document.

Pitfalls to avoid: Don't generate newsletters for all users in a single trigger invocation — batch them to avoid timeout limits on Atlas Triggers. Embed the user's interests as a concatenated string for the vector query, not as separate queries per interest. Add deduplication — track which articles have been sent to which users in a delivery_log collection to avoid repeating content across newsletters. Store the generated newsletter content so users can view past issues on a web archive without re-generating them.
